data.title: UPS: Rising Oil Prices Reveal How Fragile The Carrier's Profitability Levels Remain
data.description:
Good investments have to survive the test of time. While many companies perform well during good times, an extended sell-off is often a good way to stress-test a financial choice. Even though economic growth rates remain stable, most corporations are no longer able to rely on what have shown to be unreliable price increases over the last several years, and many of these companies are seeing minimal organic growth as well.

Few corporations have struggled more since COVID and the pandemic era shutdowns ended than United Parcel Services (UPS). The carrier saw record-high revenues in 2021 and 2022, when UPS made nearly $100 billion that year.

UPS has offered investors total returns of negative 29.26 percent over the last 5 years, while the S&P 500 (SPY) has offered investors total returns of 75.17 percent during the same time period. The carrier has consistently and significantly underperformed the broader indexes for several years now.

I last wrote about the United Parcel Services in January of 2026, and I rated the company a strong sell. Today, I am reiterating my strong sell rating of the carrier. The company's turnaround plan is not showing substantive progress of achieving the core goal of both increasing margins while also driving substantive revenue and earnings growth. The recent price shock in the oil market also further shows how vulnerable the company's fragile profitability levels are to rising costs as well. UPS still looks overvalued.

UPS reported fourth-quarter earnings per share normalized actual $2.38 and revenue actual of $24.48 billion, beating lowered expectations of nearly $24 billion and $2.20 a share. The United Parcel Services gets nearly 76 percent of the company's revenues from the US market and 67 percent of revenues from domestic shipping within the United States. The company also stated domestic revenue per piece increased by 8.3 percent while the carrier's volumes predictably declined as expected, but the drop in volume in part led to lower revenues on a year-to-year basis by nearly $1.4 billion. UPS's operating margin was 8.5 percent within this region.

The most important metrics for the carrier are revenue per piece and shipping volumes. While UPS was able to drive international revenue growth of 2.5 percent on a year-to-year basis, primarily by increasing revenue per piece by 7.1 percent. The company's international operating margin was 17.5 percent.

UPS also guided to revenue in 2026 of $89.7 billion, and analysts are expecting the carrier to earn between $7.07 and $7.21 a share for this year. Management also stated the company plans to spend nearly $3 billion this year as part of a plan to increase efficiency by moving more to automation. The carrier closed 93 buildings in 2025 as well.

Even though UPS has been able to drive margin expansion by reducing volumes and focusing on higher-end shipments, the company's plan is still not driving any real or substantive revenue growth in the critically important US market. UPS saw a 3.2 percent decline in year-over-year revenue in the United States primarily because the carrier saw average daily volume fall by 10.8 percent while margins in this market only increased by 8.3 percent. Management's turnaround plan is still not driving any revenue growth in the United States market, where the company gets nearly two-thirds of the carrier's overall earnings.

UPS's revenues peaked in September of 2022 at nearly $101 billion, and the company's 12-month trailing revenues have been in consistent decline since that time.

The company's 12-month trailing earnings per share have also been in consistent decline since December of 2022, when UPS earned $13.20 a share on a 12-month trailing basis.

UPS's overall net margins have also risen only slightly over the last year despite the company's focus on profitability. The company's net margin of 6.28 percent remains below the 10-year average for the carrier as well.

While UPS remains committed to returning cash to shareholders with buybacks and the dividend, which management has made clear the company is not going to cut, the company's long-term debt has risen significantly over the last decade as well. While the carrier returned $6.4 billion to shareholders in 2025 with buybacks and dividends, UPS has approximately $1.3 billion remaining that was authorized for share buybacks from a 2023 plan. The company expects to generate nearly $6.5 billion in free cash flow this year.

UPS currently has nearly $22.8 billion in long-term debt despite having a market cap of just $83.19 billion.

The carrier is also impacted by rising fuel costs, which have resulted from the current conflict between the US and Iran. The company relied on purchased transportation and third-party carriers last year, which made up nearly 20 percent of UPS's costs, and those expenses will obviously rise with diesel and fuel prices increasing. The United Postal Service is still seeing revenues decline while earnings and margins stagnate. These current challenges make the carrier's business model vulnerable to even moderate margin compression, which should result from rising energy prices.

This is why UPS looks overvalued at 14x expected forward earnings. Even though the company's peer, FedEx (FDX) corporation, trades at 20.85x projected forward earnings, this carrier has consistently shown substantive earnings growth over the last 6 quarters expected to more than double the company's earnings by 2031. While UPS's 5-year average valuation is 15.80x projected forward earnings, UPS's earnings and revenue growth have flatlined right now, and analyst expectations for consistent double-digit earnings growth moving forward are likely too optimistic since the company has not been able to increase revenues in key domestic markets by focusing on higher-margin shipments as part of the carrier's turnaround plan.

UPS is expected to grow revenues at just 4-6 percent over the next five years, and while some analysts are projecting low double-digit earnings growth moving forward, the company's falling shipping volumes has offset the increase in margins management's plan is focusing on. The carrier continues to see stagnant earnings per share as the company face challenges and difficult comparisons to recent record earnings coming out of COVID.

Still, all investment theses have risks, and UPS continues to focus on moving towards automation to reduce labor costs and streamline operations. The company's plan may eventually drive more substantive margin expansion. The war with Iran and oil prices are also volatile, an end to the conflict could see energy costs return to prewar levels. The carrier has also been able to maintain the dividend even during the recent turnaround plan. Management has clearly shown that the company is committed to maximizing shareholder returns with buybacks and payouts.

UPS was one of the better-performing companies in the market for some time, but the carrier has not found a way to drive significant and consistent revenue and earnings growth over the last 3 years, and management's current plan is facing challenges. While United Parcel Services has driven margin expansion domestically and earnings growth overseas, management is still struggling to deliver substantive revenue and earnings growth in the key US market.

data.title: Rigetti: Still Overvalued But Fundamentals Have Improved (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I have covered Rigetti Computing (RGTI) before, where I outlined the company’s background in detail, explained why I didn’t understand all the excitement about the company, and why I did consider it a strong sell. Since then, the stock has fallen by a 50% and has almost reached my $11 fair value; however, most of this fall is due to a reduction in the quantum valuations, which is why I further reduced its fair value to $8 per share.

Despite this I upgrade the stock to a sell because lately it is achieving more contracts.

Background

Rigetti Computing is a quantum computing company founded in 2013. The company follows a full stack approach, developing superconducting qubit based processors in its in house Fab-1, the only dedicated and integrated quantum foundry, and selling cloud access through Quantum Cloud Services.

Rigetti bases its quantum technology on superconducting qubits, as it considers that offer fast gate speeds and modular architectures based on semiconductor manufacturing. However, those advantages still come with meaningful disadvantages around coherence, complexity and temperature requirements because to achieve superconducting qubits, the electrical circuits must be cooled to near absolute zero.

These difficulties have already been showed by several delays with the last one this year. In January management delayed its Cepheus-1-108Q and said the system was expected to reach general availability around the end of the first quarter of 2026. Even during the Q4 conference call at the beginning of March management said:

Our near-term priority is completing deployment of the 108-qubit system at 99.5% median 2-qubit gate fidelity, which we expect around the end of March. Beyond that, our focus is to deploy a system with more than 150-qubits with an anticipated 99.7% median 2-qubit gate fidelity around end of December 2026.

Given that it is now April and the company has not announced that milestone, it is fair to assume the timeline has been delayed again, and I expect that the 150 qubits system by December 2026 will also be delayed.

However, that does not mean there has been no progress as the company has achieved some new contracts. This increase in contract announcements is important because one of the original bearish points was the lack of meaningful commercial traction; however, for now the demand is still concentrated in early stage research deployments for universities and research institutions, keeping revenues small and volatile.

Financial Position and Valuation

Rigetti has a comfortable financial position with around $590 million in cash or equivalents and no debt at the end of Q4. The increase in cash compared to Q3 was due to the exercise of 4 million warrants, which generated proceeds of around $45 million. However, the Q4 cash burn was approximately $14 million as the last quarter did not show a meaningful improvement in the income statement.

Rigetti reported Q4 2025 revenue of $1.9 million, which is the same as the previous quarter and lower than previous year’s $2.3 million. This is because the company is still very dependent on the timing of deliveries of its few contracts.

While I am more optimistic about 2026 revenues due to the revenue recognition of two contracts, valuation remains too expensive.

In the Q4 conference call the CEO said that the revenue recognition for the $5.7 million Novera sales would be split between the first and second quarters of 2026, while the $8.4 million C-DAC order is expected to be recognized in the second semester. Those two contracts alone will represent more than the company’s 2025 revenue.

However, even assuming that the 2026 revenue reaches $17.5 million, its $4.6 billion valuation is difficult to justify.

For valuing the leader in the sector IonQ (IONQ), I used a forward 100xP/S ratio (though I believe Infleqtion (INFQ) could be the leader in the future). I don’t believe Rigetti deserves a premium over IonQ, so if I value the company with a 100xP/S, its valuation should be $1.75 billion, or around $5 per share.

However, for some reason Rigetti tends to trade at a premium, even assuming a 150x P/S its market cap should be $2.6 billion, implying a fair value around $8 per share.

Risks

The main risk of shorting Rigetti is that if interest in quantum stocks rises again, the stock will rise regardless of fundamentals. Valuation has come down a lot as it was trading above 1,000xP/S when I published the initial article, but it provides perspective where it could go.

In the short term there is also the risk that the market rises following the announcement of the 108 qubit system which could help change the narrative to a more positive one.

Finally, if the positive contract momentum continues it could be perceived positively by the market, potentially leading to a higher valuation.

Conclusion

Rigetti is still too expensive given the future revenues it could achieve in the medium term and the continued delays in its products. The company has still to demonstrate that it could scale its quantum technology before it deserves any premium valuation.

Despite nearly reaching my previous fair value, quantum stocks have seen their ratios come down meaningfully so I have updated my fair value to $8 per share, still representing a substantial downside.

However, I am upgrading the stock rating to a sell from a strong sell due to higher contract momentum.

data.title: Robinhood: World-Class Entry Point (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Robinhood (HOOD) stock is down by -25% since my last analysis, in which I issued a Hold rating on the company. However, now there's evidence that we could be set up at world-class entry point. I admit that the equity is valued speculatively, but I'm willing to overlook that on the premise that HOOD stock looks to track Bitcoin (BTC) broadly, as well as software. Therefore, once the crypto winter ends, I see it as quite logical that sentiment for HOOD stock will begin to rise. It's not a value investment, but it does have decent growth and is trading down from its prior highs significantly. To be specific, Hood is 55% below its all-time high. I rate the stock a Buy.

Business Fundamentals

Robinhood's growth positioning is elite, even if the fundamental value isn't. FY25 revenue grew 52%, adjusted EBITDA grew 76%, and adjusted EBITDA margin expanded by 8 percentage points. 11 of the company's businesses were already above a $100M annualized run-rate by the end of 2025.

Furthermore, platform assets in FY25 were up by about 67% year-over-year (not all organic), with last-twelve-month net deposits compounding at about a 35% rate relative to the prior asset base. Moreover, Q4 ARPU increased by 16%, Gold subscribers grew by 58%, and retirement AUC grew 102%. This is fundamentally well-diversified business that is growing across several fronts.

Robinhood stock trades with crypto because the market still sees it as a liquid crypto-adjacent risk-appetite proxy. Crypto was about 20% of Robinhood's 2025 revenue, and its crypto market share rose 63% year-over-year. Unfortunately, unlike a Bitcoin treasury like Strategy (MSTR), or direct crypto, Robinhood stock is currently trading like a speculative sentiment asset even though it has a reliable and diversified operating business.

Valuation

Most readers are not going to like this valuation strategy, but I'm going to do it anyway because you have to break a few traditions to beat the herd who can't live without them. Even though HOOD has an SA Quant "Sell" rating, and a Quant valuation grade of D-, I'm bullish on the technicals. I know, I know, that's not a reliable long-term strategy for fundamentalists. But it has its place in a portfolio geared toward convexity and portfolio insurance in other vectors. For example, with 37% in cash and cash equivalents, even if a recession occurred now, which I don't think it will, I'd be well-off in the downturn to deploy into value. I can afford to own some high-convexity "speculative" positions like HOOD.

The weekly RSI has just closed-in on essentially oversold. The price action of HOOD has also mimicked BTC broadly. And the business, though fundamentally "overvalued," is growing well, popular with customers, and has a reliable long-term trajectory. To me, these are the simple ingredients of a strong trade. I see it as very likely that HOOD will rise when BTC rises. It's that simple.

We can argue all we want about the "more technical aspects," the "balance sheet," the "intrinsic discounted value of future cash flows," and all other parameters, which I already understand. It won't make you any more cash! Instead, I come back to basics. Relative value, RSIs, growth trajectory, and then if I want to be conservative, I look for a margin of safety. HOOD is not conservative.

If the company delivers $3 in normalized EPS in December 2027, then the stock price will likely be $105 in December 2026 if the forward P/E is 35, up only marginally from the present 31.5. In other words, as the stock is now $69, a +52% return.

Risks

The HOOD = BTC proxy narrative is less clean that it sounds on paper in reality. Crypto was 20% of FY25 revenue, and management still maintains that the business is diversifying across many lines. If diversification keeps working, HOOD may stop giving the clean one-for-one BTC beta this thesis is relying on, but I still thinks it's valid for now.

Right now, the revenue model is still cyclical and concentrated, with PFOF economics. 59% of FY25 revenue was transaction-based, and 55% of total net revenue consisted of transaction-based revenues paid by market makers and exchanges. Citadel alone represented 13% of net revenue. This creates volatility risks in the price and makes a low entry point, like now, more opportune.

Finally, management guided 2026 adjusted opex and SBC to $2.6B-$2.7B, up 18% at the midpoint, with more than half of that growth going into new and scaling businesses. If adoption lags, the multiple can compress before the revenue shows up and cap the upside thesis presented here.

Conclusion: Buy

Is HOOD one of the best stocks on the market? No, not in my opinion. But is it one of the best entry points right now for somewhat speculative returns that are not heavy on margin of safety but high on technical positive probability outcome? Yes. Or to be more blunt, HOOD mimics crypto, and crypto winter is over soon and we'll be heading into spring.

Some people might not like the stock, and that's fine. You stick to your discounted cash flows and your advanced technical analysis, that's fine. If it works for you, it works for you. This is what works for me. We're all entitled to our own views and strategies. And I've been hitting 50-60% annual returns recently, so I'm not planning on changing course any time soon. Onwards and upwards.

On a closing and more positive note, I'm fond of HOOD's management. After watching several interviews with the CEO, I'm convinced that the business is in good hands. Robinhood isn't my main broker, but I do use it for one portfolio, and you guessed it, it's the one most heavily oriented to crypto. In fact, it's a crypto treasury portfolio. HOOD does the job well. The company's financial statements and forward growth look strong, and the technicals on the stock price right now look primed for a world-class entry point.

data.title: Palantir: Winner Of The AI War
data.description:
Following the war in Iran, aerospace and defense stocks have not fared well but there is one company that has taken the spotlight in the war both in positive and negative sense and that company is Palantir Technologies (PLTR). The stock has gained 8.7% since my last report. In this report, I pay closer attention to how Palantir’s product stack is used as the current war in Iran provides large-scale evidence of how Palantir’s stack scales the war capabilities.

Note: War often comes with an unmeasurable amount of human tragedy and a fair bit of geopolitics. While I am cognizant of these factors, this report does not intend to discuss either the human tragedy or the politics behind the most recent combat action but aims to provide qualitative analysis of the role of Palantir’s product stack in the war.

Palantir’s Modular Set Up Shines

Palantir product suite is often misunderstood because it is not a set of isolated products, but a layered software stack where each component plays a distinct role. Understanding how these pieces connect is of paramount importance to understand the product value proposition.

  1. Palantir Foundry can be seen as the data backbone integrating data from disparate sources, builds a data model and enables analytics and model development.
  2. Palantir Gotham can be seen as the operational brain and operational decision platform fusing multiple intelligence streams into an operational picture allowing for mission planning and execution. Whereas Foundry organizes data, Gotham fuses it and turns its into actionable operations.
  3. Palantir Apollo allows for the continuous delivery of software even in disconnected environments.
  4. Pantar Artificial Intelligence Platform or AIP is the AI orchestration layer that sits on top of Foundry and Gotham and introduces AI-driven decision making.
  5. Maven Smart System is a mission-specific AI application built on top of the stack used for analyzing drone and sensor data, object detection and target tracking and prioritization.

So, we have full stack that integrates data through Foundry, provides operational real-time context through Gotham, runs AI enabled analysis and models through AIP while Maven performs specialized tasks and Apollo is the software distribution layer. Each part provides a key function. Maven Smart System is in focus in Iran, but it actually needs all modules to execute its tasks. Maven Smart Systems is the result of Project Maven which was launched in 2017 to accelerate adoption of machine learning and data integration in the U.S. military intelligence layer. In 2018, the purpose of the project was altered to AI-enabled analysis of video with a further step up towards combat commands.

The system capabilities have evolved over time with evolving AI implementation allowing the number of targets per day to rise from less than 100 to 1,000 after computer vision system implementation. Computer Vision System is simply processing images and video in a way a human would and assign targets based on that. Language learning models further increased the capability to 5,000 targets per day with future iterations expected to push target acquisition to 1,000 per hour. What it essentially does is removing the intelligence and decision making bottleneck and that allowed the US and Israel to strike 2,000 targets within 48 hours. As a reference, in the war in Iraq in 2003 human analysts were able to pin point about 100 targets per day on cycle times of 24-72 hrs. Through AI and automation and parallelized work flow, the US can now target over 1,000 targets per day clearly showing the shift from analysis and targeting to strike capacity.

In 2025, NATO also selected Maven and I believe it is not a weird thing that NATO selected Maven as Maven provides a real edge that especially in the early phase of the war can result in targeted strikes that can determine the outcome of a war.

There Is A Negative On Maven

One reason why target cycles were substantially longer 13 years ago was the human approach towards target setting as well as verification in a largely sequential set up. Maven is parallelized and can target much faster, but it also becomes more difficult to correct wrong target setting as also has happen in Iran. When wrong targets are being set, it bears the question who carries responsibility. Furthermore, because the high speed and automated character of Maven, it can also distance militaries from gravity of life-and-death decisions. So, there is the risk of automation bias trusting deeply on a system that may still occasionally mistarget. Other risks include perception of the authority of the platform leading to reduced willingness to challenge AI assigned targets as well as cognitive offloading and moral disengagement and deskilling.

So, as target cycles compress from days/hours to minutes, the risk is not only faster warfare which can be a benefit but more the gradual shift from human judgement to algorithmic authority raising concerns about where meaningful human control can be preserved in life-and-death situations. So, where things may collide is human-in-the-loop (human approves strike) versus human-on-the-loop (human acts as supervisor without deep evaluation of decisions).

So, while Palantir really seems to provide militaries with an edge it also has to make sure that its contracts are structured in such a way that with a reasonable framework the responsibility of the action taken based on the outcome provided by Maven is largely in the realm of the party using the system. If that is not the case, it puts Palantir and its investors in a high-risk position.

Palantir Technologies Is Valued Well For A Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Palantir, we note that EBITDA estimates for 2026-2030 have increased by 3.9% driven by a growth acceleration expected in 2029 as adoption accelerates. Free cash flow estimates have improved by 46.9% driven by the company having an extremely high cash conversion and operating leverage. At almost 153x EV/EBITDA, Palantir Technologies is not cheap. However, I have implemented a elite growth valuation model that I discussed in a prior report and from there I derived a 83.6x EV/EBITDA median which is still high but fully values the combination of margin and growth. Interestingly, we see that the stock is fairly valued for 2026 and perhaps that provides us with an indication that while most investors do not know according to which set of principles the stock is valued, the market in aggregate converges to a valuation approach in line with the elite growth model. This would also imply around 49% upside with a $221.86 price target when incorporating 2027 earnings. So, I am maintaining my buy rating.

For Palantir, I expect that margins have an asymptote around 60% EBITDA margins with very strong cash flows and free cash flow conversion. Ultimately that would indicate a $47 billion cash pile with no meaningful debt and an enterprise value of more than $1 trillion.

Conclusion: Palantir Is Proving Its Value In War

I believe that the war in Iran and the deployment of Palantir’s Maven Smart System shows how AI and automation can move the strike bottle necks from target setting to equipment being the bottleneck and it also provides an indication on how the strike capability substantially increases which can alter the outcome of wars. I do believe that with the increased strike capability, Iran which has shown to be a tough adversary has been made less effective. If target setting would have happened at substantially lower pace, US soldiers as well as countries in the Middle East would likely have been even more exposed than they are today. Palantir is showing the way to impact war through AI and right now there is other company with that capability. At the same time, I do believe that investors need to be aware of the risks that come with AI-enabled targeting and decision making.

data.title: Motorola Solutions: Strong Performance And A Modest Premium
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Motorola Solutions (MSI) appears to be trading for a small premium at this time, while offering a high-single-digit return potential. As such I believe a HOLD rating is appropriate, despite solid financial metrics and steady dividend growth. I'd prefer a little more margin of safety before I consider MSI.

Company Description

Motorola Solutions is a provider of critical communication infrastructure, devices and software, specifically for public safety and municipal customers. The company reports through two segments, Products and Systems integration which focuses hardware related products like video security, access systems, along with radio and communication systems. The other segment is Software & Services which earns revenue through contracts for maintenance, managed services, dispatching and analytic related products. MSI has a current market cap of more than $70B and has more than 23,000 employees around the globe. Over the past decade Motorola Solutions has seen price appreciation more than double that of the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY).

Quality Financial Metrics

Let's take a look at some key quantitative financial metrics that all high-quality businesses possess. I'll be looking for a growing revenue stream, stable margins, and a healthy return on invested capital.

The revenue per share has been incredibly consistent with average growth between 8-10% annually. The company saw a minor setback during the pandemic where the RPS declined by almost 8%, but it followed up 2020 with double-digit growth in 2021 and 2022. The growth has slowed minimally since then, but is still typically in the high-single-digit range.

The gross profit margin has been expanding ever so slightly over the past ten years. Prior to 2020 this metric was a tick below 50%, but over the past two fiscal years, as the company has focused on higher margin software products, the GPM has eclipsed the 50% mark. I expect this metric to expand as the company shifts to a more recurring and predictable business model, particularly in the Software and Services segment.

Lastly, the return on invested capital has seen minimal volatility during the last decade, with the exception of 2017. Between 2018 and 2022, the ROIC was generally in the mid to upper teens, and briefly surpassed the 20% mark in 2023 and 2024. This metric slipped last fiscal year, but was still respectable at almost 16%, close to my usual threshold of 20%.

Dividend Data

Motorola Solutions has increased its dividend for almost 15 years and pays a quarterly dividend of $1.21 per share, or $4.64 annually, equating to a forward dividend yield of 1.1%. In November of last year the company announced an 11% dividend hike, which was in-line with the 3-, 5-, and 10-year CAGRs which are all slightly above 11%. Although the company has grown its dividend by double-digits for a number of years, the payout ratio is still at a very respectable level. In fact, this metric has been slowly declining, peaking at 46% during the pandemic, and dropping to less than 34% in fiscal year 2025. To be honest, the company has reliably grown its dividend by around 10%, and I see no reason this shouldn't continue for the foreseeable future.

Recent Earnings

In mid-February Motorola Solutions announced its Q4 2025 earnings with Non-GAAP earnings per share of $4.59, north of the consensus and an annual jump of 14%. Revenue for the quarter was $3.4B, which also exceeded analyst expectations by $60M, and was a year-over-year increase of more than 13%. Additionally, GAAP earnings per share came in at $3.86, beating the street's estimate by $0.19.

Revenue for the Products & Systems Integration segment saw a healthy increase of 11%, rising from $1.95B to $2.16B with strong demand in the Mission Critical Networks division as well as Video Security products. Operating earnings also grew, increasing by $47M compared to the same quarter a year ago, although the margin contracted slightly, dipping about 60 bps.

The higher margin Software and Services segment saw massive growth across all three business units, with total revenue increasing 15%, or $160M. The biggest increase came from the Command Center division which earned the highest YoY growth of almost 20%, driven by cloud-based Solutions, AI features, and the APX Next application. Video Services and MCN grew by close to double-digits, but were also aided by acquisitions which contributed $37M for the quarter, while FX tailwinds added another $10M. Additionally, GAAP operating earnings rose $83M, or 30%, while the operating margin expanded a whopping 340 bps.

The company also ended the fiscal year with a record backlog of $15.7B, a $1B jump relative to the end of FY24, with a little less than half of that resulting from positive FX rates. Moreover, the Software segment had its backlog rise by $1.4B, or about 13%, while the Products and Systems segment incurred a backlog decline of 8%, or slightly more than $320M.

And finally, MSI returned almost $575M to shareholders during Q4, as the company paid more than $180M in dividends, and acquired close to $500M of its stock for an average price that's about 8% lower than its current share price of $439.

The market had a positive reaction to the earnings call, which can be read/listened to here, as shares of the stock jumped around 8% the day following the release. However, since then the stock has retreated a few percentage points, likely, the result of negative market sentiment in recent weeks surrounding the Iran situation.

Management also provided Q1 2026 guidance where they are projecting revenue growth of 6.5% at the midpoint, with normalized earnings per share of between $3.20 and $3.25.

Valuation

The final piece of this puzzle is to determine if MSI is trading for an attractive valuation, and to do so, let's examine it from a free cash flow perspective.

I estimate a stock's implied fair value based on its free cash flow yield, and specifically how the current yield compares to its historical average. This helps me gauge how much investors have typically been willing to pay for a company's free cash flow and how attractive the stock looks today relative to that history. The analysis is calculated on 12 years of daily historical price data, capturing the rolling 5-year average free cash flow yield for the past 7 years. The valuation chart below juxtaposes MSI's actual share price and the fair value range calculated using this methodology.

The key assumption behind this valuation model is that over iterations of rolling 5-year periods, on average, investors have established an accurate fair price for the stock.

But since every valuation is an estimate, there is a +/-10% margin of error applied to the fair value range

My custom free cash flow valuation model indicates MSI is trading for a slight premium at this time, with a current share price of $439, and fair price close to $403, equating to a possible 9% overvaluation. As you can see coming out the pandemic, the company has seen consistent FCF growth with some years better than others, but the positive trajectory is always there. The company boasts a decent estimated forward return potential of 8.34%, which consists of a forward dividend yield of 1.1%, a return to fair value factor of -1.74%, and a long-term expected earnings growth rate of nearly 9%.

Final Thoughts

Motorola Solutions has vastly outperformed the broad market over the past 10 years. The company posts consistent revenue growth, reliable margins, and a solid return on invested capital. MSI has grown its dividend for almost 15 years, and although it doesn't yield much it does offer solid dividend growth, coupled with a declining payout ratio. The company had another solid quarter, with strong growth in both segments, and projects to have an encouraging start to FY26. The valuation is a little pricey at this time, and the forward return potential is a little below my typical threshold of 10%. As a result of these factors, I am going to rate MSI a HOLD, as I'd like to see the stock price pullback a little more in order to give me a better margin of safety if I were to initiate a position.

data.title: "Breakthrough Defense Budget"
data.description:
The editors of the Wall Street Journal signal that the new Trump administration budget is "Trump's Breakthrough Defense Budget."

"Washington has for years given priority to spending on butter over guns. The result is a shrunken military industrial base that has been exposed by wars in Iran and Ukraine."

"Credit then to President Trump, who is meeting the moment by proposing a new and record increase in national defense."

A Wall Street Journal article on the defense budget makes clear that "Trump promised a $1.5 trillion military request in January, before the U.S. launched large-scale strikes on Iran."

This little bit of information causes us to add even more government spending to the agenda for next year's military budget.

The proposed budget for 2027 will move the defense spending to "roughly double what it was in 2021 and more than 40 percent higher than this year, including the supplemental $155 billion that Congress provided in last summer's bill."

"Defense spending has fallen to 3 percent as a share of GDP, compared to some 6 percent in the 1980s and 8 percent in the 1960s."

"The White House budget would increase this to 4.5 percent, which is close to the 5 percent goal that the President has set for NATO members."

The president wants more defense spending. And, one way or another, the president is going to get more defense spending.

My concern with this added spending is about how the added spending is going to be financed. And, the financing of the debt can have serious ramifications concerning how much of the debt is going to be monetized by the Federal Reserve.

This further leads to a question about the ability of the Federal Reserve to conduct monetary policy in the way it has been operating since 2009.

In 2009, Fed Chair Ben Bernanke introduced a new way to conduct monetary policy. In this "new approach," the policymakers focused on setting out a program for how fast they would add...or subtract...securities from Fed's securities bought outright.

The Federal Reserve has followed this approach to monetary policy since 2009 and, I think...along with a lot of other people...that the Fed has done a very good job in applying monetary policy.

Since the third quarter of 2009, the U.S. economy has grown at a compound rate of 2.4 percent, inflation has been relatively modest, and unemployment in the U.S. has remained right around or just above 4.0 percent, a very good number historically.

In December of 2025, the Federal Reserve moved into a round of "quantitative easing," its fifth round of quantitative easing since the program started.

The Fed has conducted just one round of quantitative tightening. This round went from March 2022 through November 2025.

Since the Fed introduced this new approach to conducting monetary policy, the U.S. economy has only experienced one economic recession, and it was the shortest one in history...only two months...February and March of 2020.

The most important thing about conducting this policy was the requirement to start out...and maintain...a plan about how much the Fed would add to its securities portfolio...or subtract from its securities portfolio...going forward.

Even though the United States produced substantial budget deficits and, consequently, had to bring lots of securities to market, the Federal Reserve has been able to manage its quantitative easing or quantitative tightening without any major problems.

The question that exists now is that with the pickup in government spending on defense, how much impact will the rising amount of debt have on the Fed's ability to calmly manage the amount of debt that must be raised in the open-market.

I have tried to start a discussion on this issue in some of my recent posts.

The Federal Reserve seems to have moved from a stance of quantitative tightening to one of quantitative easing in December 2025. In December 2025, and in January and February 2026, the Federal Reserve started to add securities to its portfolio once again.

The Iran "war" began on February 28, 2026, and it appears as if the Fed continued to add securities to its portfolio, but at a slightly faster rate.

This is discussed in my last post, Federal Reserve Watch: Portfolio Takes Off.

This could indicate that the "war" and the spending on the "war" might be impacting monetary policy.

This is an important question to be able to answer.

President Trump had not been happy with the way that the Federal Reserve, under the leadership of Chair Jerome Powell, was conducting policy.

President Trump wanted the Fed to produce lower interest rates and faster reserve growth in the banking system.

Powell and the Federal Reserve wanted to stay with their current policy stance and control the amount of securities that were added to the Fed's securities portfolio.

Question: is this effort to produce government defense spending a methodology to force the Federal Reserve to support the added securities that will be coming to market and provide the banking system with more "excess reserves" than the Federal Reserve would have allowed otherwise?

Right now, the question cannot be answered.

It looks like the Federal Reserve could be buying more securities than it had originally planned to buy.

But, it is too early to really come to a conclusion on this point.

The defense budget is a "breakthrough" anyway!

The 2027 budget lays out a "new" worldview.

Whether or not the 2027 budget is a proposed means to intrude on the monetary policy of the Federal Reserve is another question that must be answered going forward.

In this view, the "defense" budget could be thought of as an "offense" budget.

Some of the questions could be answered sooner than we might expect them...so stay tuned.

data.title: Intel: The Turnaround Is Alive But The Stock Has Priced It In
data.description:
Introduction

It has been a while since I last reviewed Intel (INTC), which was at one point the most interesting story in the stock market. Since my last Buy rating, the company has lost around 2% of its value, actually beating the broad S&P 500. It has continued on its turnaround and I’ll dive into the current dynamics to see if Intel can claw its way back to the top.

Current Dynamics

I’ll first start with a review of Lip-Bu Tan’s leadership as I believe that he is the right man for the job, considering that he was recruited to solve the execution gaps that had negatively impacted Intel for more than a decade. He is essentially capacity-gating, which is a refusal to build speculative factory space without firm external customer commitments. More so, the firm is even more tilting toward a service-based foundry model with examples in the data center and AI segment, in which Tan focuses resources on the 16-channel Diamond Rapids and accelerates the introduction of Coral Rapids, which reintroduces multi-threading back into the server roadmap. In all, Tan is essentially betting on bringing back the engineering-first environment that made Intel so dominant to begin with.

2026 is also centered on the 18A process node, which has officially moved into high-volume manufacturing in Arizona and Oregon, and this node will be key to the company’s turnaround. It will introduce new tech like RibbonFET gate-all-around transistors as well as PowerVia backside power delivery. These are not set to be deployed by TSMC (TSM) until the end of the year or even 2027. The commercial use, Panther Lake, is designed to deliver a 50% multi-threaded performance leap and massive gains in its NPU for local AI workloads. This platform is set to power over 200 designs from global OEMs, making it the most broadly adopted AI pc platform in the company’s history.

Though right now, Intel is surfing on a normalization phase in terms of yields, as 18A yields are now between 65% and 75% while they stood at around 55% back in mid 2025. Though these are sufficient for the Panther Lake launch, they still remain highly below the 80%+ threshold required for the Foundry to reach its 40% gross margin target. That’s why management has stated that reaching a 70%+ yield for high-margin chips is a non-negotiable for the company’s financial recovery.

On the x86 side, there is still a supply crunch going on as the demand for AI data centers has continued to outpace the company’s manufacturing capacity. That’s why management stated that they were caught off guard by the sheer size of demand for the server CPUs, thus pushing Intel to prioritize Xeon processors over PC silicon. Though this is great in terms of unit margins, this also accelerates the competitive shift toward AMD (AMD) and ARM (ARM) alternatives. AMD has reached a record x86 market share with around 36.4% of the desktop market and 28.8% of the server market, telling us that AMD is successfully capturing the higher-margin enthusiast and performance segments while Intel struggles with entry-level and mainstream supply.

On the foundry side, the long-term prospects are anchored with the 14A process node, which is scheduled for risk production in late 2027 and volume manufacturing in 2028. Since January, the company has released version 0.5 of the 14A process Design Kit to lead customers. Though Intel currently has no committed external customers for 14A, CEO Lip-Bu Tan reported initial feedback and active engagements with two potential anchor clients. More so the company is unwilling to guarantee capacity without firm contracts to avoid repeating the over-expansion errors of the 10nm era.

Valuation

As Intel is currently undergoing its transition through a high-intensity capital deployment phase, we should see a recovery in earnings through 2027 and 2028. But at the moment, most basic valuation metrics don't do the job. It currently has an EV/EBITDA of 20.77, far below AMD at 50.41 and Broadcom (AVGO) at 41.27. Beyond that, I’ve put out a sum of the part analysis as well as a scenario analysis to see if $50 a share can still offer a strong margin of safety. Though that has not been the case in my analysis, as Intel seems overvalued.

I’ve put an EV/EBITDA multiple on the CCG side at 7x and DCAI at 14x, considering the immense growth on that side, though Intel Foundry continues to bleed money. This all gives a fair value per share at $29.54, while on a scenario basis with a tilt more toward a bullish stance, the probability weighted target price stands at $27.58, implying a 45.2% downside. Though I understood that Intel’s story is heavily hinged on the capacity of its foundry to become competitive against TSMC.

I will be closely monitoring the advancement of the new GPU division, in which Intel is developing clean-sheet architectures like Falcon Shores and Crescent Island, which are built on 14A and optimized specifically for generative AI workloads. On the server market, Intel has partnered with Nvidia to integrate Xeon processors directly with NVLink technology in order to target a 45% market share in client devices.

Risks

The foundry segment continues to be a massive financial drag on profitability, as it reported an operating loss of $2.51B in Q4 of 2025, and this trend is expected to continue through the near future.

The success of the turnaround is also heavily hinged on the 18A process node, in which management has stated that reaching the 70% yield threshold is a non-negotiable for the node’s economic viability, as it would mean a lower than 40% gross margin.

Conclusion

I remain a believer in what Lip-Bu Tan is building at Intel. The next 6 months will be pivotal to see if the firm can find an anchor customer to support the current mission to regain the engineering crown in 2028. If the 18A yields normalize as projected and the Panther Lake launch translates into significant OEM volume, the firm will have established the first sustainable and leading-edge foundry in the US. But the road ahead remains steep and thus I downgrade my rating to a Hold for Intel.

data.title: FOMC To Release Minutes Of March Meeting, Delta Air Lines Earnings In Focus
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street's major market averages ended mixed on Thursday as markets assessed President Donald Trump's address to the nation and the latest developments in the Middle East conflict. Trump said the U.S. will strike Iran "extremely hard" over the next two to three weeks, warning the country would be pushed "back to the stone ages," though he indicated talks with Tehran remain ongoing. On a sector-by-sector basis, five of the 11 S&P segments concluded in the red, with consumer discretionary at the bottom. At the other end of the spectrum, real estate was the strongest performer.

The next week will feature a slew of important economic data, beginning with ISM Non-Manufacturing PMI, business activity data for March on Monday. The FOMC's minutes of the meeting will be released on Wednesday, with Fed Governor Philip Jefferson's speech scheduled for the same day. The quarterly GDP data, PCR price index data, and jobless claims data are due on Thursday. Inflation numbers along with the Fed's balance sheet will be released on Friday.

Delta Air Lines (DAL), Applied Digital (APLD), and BlackBerry (BB) are among the companies reporting their results next week.

_______________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday: Levi Strauss (LEVI). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday: Delta Air Lines, Applied Digital. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday: BlackBerry. See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: CoreWeave (CRWV) and Cytokinetics (CYTK) are set up for a volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include KORE Group (KORE), Apellis Pharma (APLS), and Tilly's (TLYS). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Service Properties (SVC), SANUWAVE Health (SNWV), and MDxHealth SA (MDXH). Short interest is elevated on Sphere Entertainment (SPHR) and Serve Robotics (SERV) again.

IPO watch: No IPOs are expected to price next week and begin to trade. The IPO lockup periods expire on blocks of shares of Alliance Laundry (ALH), Phoenix Education Partners (PXED), and Leifras (LFS). The quiet period ends on PayPay (PAYP) to free up analysts to post ratings. The countdown for investors to see SpaceX's (SPACE) financials ahead of a planned June IPO will continue.

Delta Air Lines Earnings Preview: Delta Air Lines is expected to report per-share earnings of $0.60 during its first-quarter earnings scheduled for April 8. Analysts have projected a quarterly revenue of $14.86B. Investors are optimistic ahead of the earnings, especially after the company raised its Q1 revenue outlook earlier in March, citing healthy demand trends. Going into the earnings, analysts will be looking forward to the company's commentary on airport congestion tied to the recent TSA staffing strains during the government shutdown. They are also expecting remarks on DAL's newly announced partnership with Amazon to power next-generation in-flight Wi-Fi through its LEO satellite network.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Spruce Power: Cash Burn And Debt Dim Outlook
data.description:
Spruce Power's (SPRU) pivot to residential U.S. solar has helped boost its revenue profile but failed to improve profitability, with the firm burning through its cash reserves at a time when capital looks set to remain in a risk-off mode. SPRU reported fiscal 2025 fourth quarter revenue of $24.02 million, up 18.7% over its year-ago comp. The growth seen was on the back of the company's $132.5 million acquisition of a residential solar portfolio from NJR Clean Energy Ventures in November 2024. NJR's assets consisted of 9,800 solar systems in New Jersey that are backed with long-term lease agreements with homeowners, with an average remaining contract life of over 11 years. However, revenue dipped by 21.8% sequentially and is down from $33.2 million in the second quarter. This dip was more reflective of seasonality with solar production, with generation lower during the winter months. It's a trend that can be seen with SPRU's 3-year revenue trendline. The ticker has dipped roughly 40% from its 52-week high and is down 15% since I last covered it with a Hold rating.

SPRU's bull case is built around residential adoption of rooftop solar in a broader macro environment where policy support for the rollout of renewables has been outright hostile. The 2025 passing of the One Big Beautiful Bill ("OBBB") brought about the reversal of the clean energy tax credits of the landmark 2022 Inflation Reduction Act ("IRA"), with the 30% federal tax credit on residential solar phased out from the end of 2025. This had essentially allowed homeowners to deduct 30% of a residential solar system’s cost from their taxes. SPRU operates an as-a-service model, which allows consumers to access these systems without making a significant upfront investment. SPRU generated a fourth-quarter net loss of $6.9 million, around $0.38 per share, up from a loss of $5.9 million, or $0.32 per share, in the year-ago comp.

Growth, Liquidity, And Cash Flows

SPRU has been able to grow through prudent tuck-in acquisitions that have allowed the company to expand its portfolio. The company had 84,000 home solar assets and customer contracts across 18 U.S. states with an average remaining contract life of roughly 10 years as of the end of its fourth quarter. The combined portfolio generation was roughly 104,711 MWh of energy, down from 190,081 MWh during the third quarter due to the aforementioned seasonality.

SPRU also serviced around 60,000 third-party owned home solar systems as of the end of the fourth quarter, with its gross portfolio value at $848 million. The risk for bulls is just how much of the underlying revenue momentum will be driven by factors broadly outside of the company's control, with the weather being the core driver of this risk. The market for residential solar providers across the U.S. is dispersed, and there will be a lot more opportunities for SPRU to acquire small regional providers that allow expansion of its portfolio.

The Houston-based company had a weighted average shares outstanding balance of 18,068,059 as of the end of the fourth quarter, essentially unchanged from its prior year-ago quarter, with its stock price at $4.06, meaning the company's market cap stands at $75 million. The average daily trading volume stands at around 60,000 shares, which would limit the ability of some retail investors to build a position, as it would come with harder-to-fill orders and a larger bid-ask spread. The company has mainly leaned on debt to meet recent acquisitions, with total debt at $695.5 million as of the end of the fourth quarter. This had a blended interest rate of roughly 6.1%, with the company closing the year with a total of $93.1 million in cash.

While net loss attributable to shareholders grew from its year-ago comp, SPRU was able to push through cash from operations of $3.30 million, down from cash burn of $13.5 million in the year-ago quarter. This negative operating cash flow position will put pressure on a cash position that's seeing $13.4 million in quarterly interest payments, around 55.8% of revenue during the quarter. This large total debt position, in aggregate with the cash burn from operations, is why I've stayed on the sidelines with SPRU. The current financials are privy to seasonality, and the business isn't that attractive on a GAAP net income basis. However, bulls would be right to highlight that its cash position provides a runway of many years, especially on the back of the reduction in cash burn from operations seen during the fourth quarter. SPRU also highlighted gains in reducing its O&M and SG&A expenses, with these respectively down year-over-year by 64% and 16%. However, cash from operations remains negative, albeit with the company recording positive adjusted cash flow from operations. This non-GAAP metric is defined by SPRU as cash from operations adjusted for the recurring proceeds from both our SEMTH master lease and sales of solar energy systems. It also includes non-recurring litigation settlements. The company received $637,000 during the quarter from litigation settlements.

Conclusion

SPRU is set to face a more timid demand backdrop for residential solar in the U.S. on the expiration of the federal tax credit. This comes with its total debt balance at a high, net losses, and cash burn from operations. However, the company's revenue profile is up from a year ago, and cash burn from operations is showing a positive upward trend. I am rating SPRU as a Hold.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Ramaco Resources CAL NT 29 -- 8.375% Carry, Short-Dated, Low Duration
data.description:
Ramaco Resources, Inc. CAL NT 29 (METCZ) are senior unsecured notes of Ramaco Resources (METC), which were recently issued to raise ~$50 million (before underwriting expenses) for general corporate purposes, future investments & capex and working capital.

METCZ carry a coupon rate of 8.375% per annum, payable quarterly. With current market price of $25.2, the current yield is broadly in line with the coupon. The bonds nearly trade at par at present. The notes are maturing at November 30, 2029. Therefore, the time remaining to maturity is roughly ~3.5 years from now. These debentures are callable starting from November 2026.

However, METCZ nevertheless tend to differ from many other callable debentures, which also give the company an option to redeem the issue post predetermined timelines but in terms of stated maturity are close to perpetuity or have a very long-time horizon. However, METCZ for that matter would be maturing soon, causing modified duration, even with respect to yield to maturity, to shrink significantly. With reference to yield to call, since the redemption period would start at end-2026, modified duration reduces further. Simply put, METCZ would function more as a carry instrument with very low sensitivity to interest rate fluctuations on either side.

Considering this aspect, Ramaco's senior debentures present a noteworthy characteristics for investors in terms of duration exposure. The other way to understand this is to think of the right to call the issue prior to maturity as a call option held by the company. Naturally, this call option is valuable for the issuer. At the same time, we can think of investors as the ones who have effectively sold this call option to the issuer. Without getting into whether the initial yield offered at issuance of these securities was sufficient to compensate investors for the sold option, we shall at least assess the current stated utility of that option with reference to yield offered.

Going into this further, the longer the maturity, the more valuable is that call option for the issuer. This implies that investors in such issues, due to these redemption rights, carry asymmetric duration exposure — in times of rising interest rates, they are prone to capital losses, but do not fully participate in capital upside in times of declining rates due to the callability rights of the issuer.

In the case of METCZ however, since the maturity date is fairly short, the value of the call option is relatively limited. The same applies to the duration of these bonds, which becomes fairly low, thus reducing interest rate sensitivity. An investor, therefore, would largely derive returns from credit-related developments and the fundamental profile of the company. In that sense, ~8.375% of recurring yield does not look inadequate even if the investor does not get to participate in capital upside in case of declining interest rates.

Ramaco Resources Overview

According to Seeking Alpha summary—

Ramaco Resources, Inc. (METC) is a Delaware corporation, founded in 2015, and headquartered in Lexington, Kentucky, with additional operational offices in Charleston, West Virginia, and Sheridan, Wyoming. The company is primarily engaged in the development, operation, and sale of metallurgical coal and is advancing into the rare earth elements and critical minerals sector. Ramaco's metallurgical coal operations are strategically located in southern West Virginia and southwestern Virginia, including properties such as Elk Creek, Berwind, Knox Creek, and Maben. These sites offer geologic and logistical advantages, positioning the company's coal as among the lowest delivered-cost options for North American blast furnace steel mills, coke plants, and international metallurgical coal consumers.

Ramaco Resources saw a significant appreciation in the share price over the last year. Although since we are speaking about notes, they do not participate in the upside. Still however, any improvement in the fundamental standing of the company nevertheless reduces the cost of debt for the company, making previously issued debt more valuable to the holders. In addition, any reduction in both underlying and perceived credit risk of the issuer naturally tends to benefit the debt holders.

Needless to say, share price could have been appreciated due to many other reasons & investor expectations, which might not be indicative of improvement in the fundamental prospects of the business.

Therefore, it would be useful to analyse the capital structure along with interest expenses for Ramaco Resources. This is done not to speculate on the future trajectory of the common stock, but to assess the current safety of the underlying notes. This becomes important since the notes, by nature, do not participate in upside but are exposed to the downside.

The total common equity capital currently is at around ~$484 million. The company saw a significant increase in net borrowings for FY 2025. The borrowings were up from ~$88 million to ~$451 million. This was done largely by issuing an additional ~$300 million in senior convertible notes in November 2025.

However, the important aspect to note is that these ~$300 million worth of convertible notes carry 0% cash interest and thereby do not impact cash interest coverage of senior notes such as METCZ.

Kindly note that slight difference in the carrying balance is on account of underwriters’ expenses adjusted for unamortized debt discounts. The senior notes METCZ at present comprises of outstanding balance of ~$54.9 million and the other 2030 series notes stands at ~$61.7 million.

The effective interest rate for both the issues is roughly ~9.7%. Therefore, on the total senior notes of around ~$117 million, leaving minor accounting adjustments aside, Ramaco Resources would require ~$11–12 million in annual interest payments. The interest paid for FY25 approximated ~$8.063 million, reflecting also the partial impact of incremental borrowings. Net debt at FY24 totalled ~$88 million.

With the total cash balance of ~$440 million , I do not see any immediate issues with reference to interest servicing, even if net income happens to be negative. The current financial position of the company, at least for the time being, appears adequate for servicing the current debt. Further, the company being able to raise ~$300 million at 0% cash interest reinforces this positioning significantly. Kindly recollect, that these convertible notes were issued very recently on November, 2025. Even if the common equity stands diluted with convertible nature of those issues, for fixed capital providers, it still would be considered a positive than otherwise.

MPCEZ is rated as a buy

The senior notes of Ramaco Resources can be considered as an investment because of the current higher yield accompanied by a lower duration exposure. Sure, the current proposition won’t benefit from falling interest rates, and investors looking for vehicles which do that predominantly might not want to consider this for the time being. Leaving this qualification aside, however, when we consider risk-free yields of ~4%–4.5%, the current above 8% yield is compelling.

The factor which further favours the current proposition is the well-capitalized nature of Ramaco Resources along with balance sheet strength to sustain recurring interest obligations and principal repayments as and when they come due. In addition, shorter time to maturity decreases the uncertainty associated with future deterioration of the credit profile of the company to a meaningful extent. The debts are due to mature at around year end-2029, which places debtholders in a relatively favourable position, along with a decent ~8.375% rate of returns. The low duration exposure also offsets risks associated with elevated interest rate levels.

In my opinion, the current yield provides an adequate rate of return with reasonable safety of principal; and therefore, could be considered by investors desiring high yield exposure with shorter maturity & low duration levels.

Limitations to the buy case

The major drawback to the current thesis is again the rate cut expectations at present. The debentures, being callable in addition to already having a very short time to maturity, would not tend to benefit should interest rates decline. This might cause them to underperform debentures with a similar credit profile but higher duration exposure. The issues already trade at close to par and therefore do not suggest any meaningful mispricing or optional upside. The returns attributable would most probably be identical to yield to maturity. The fundamental risks for the company, being in the commodity segment with fluctuating earnings power, even if currently moderate, still remain, and that is the reason the company needs to pay above 8% interest to raise money. This aspect should not be disregarded altogether just because the debentures are maturing soon.

In addition, there is also a chance of the company redeeming the issue even prior to the already short maturity period, which would not only force investors to reinvest at potentially lower yields, but an investor, by virtue of being invested in this issue, might also suffer in opportunity cost should other comparable instruments see a meaningful increase in their prices.

In Conclusion

METCZ offers a fair and adequate return for investors seeking a stable and relatively high-yield option. A short time to maturity, with the issue trading at par with ~8.375% coupon yield, looks compelling for investors who do not desire to bet on duration (interest rates) and are largely looking for an avenue to park money in fixed income for a near to medium-term time frame. Those investors who also wish to benefit should rates decline, however, might want to keep this aside for the time being.
